domain: oology (common murre Uria aalge pyriform-egg cliff-ledge stability)
specific_mechanism: Murre eggs are pyriform: a long axis with one blunt and one pointed end. Birkhead (2017) showed the egg's roll-arc is wider than typical cliff ledges, so the original 'narrow-roll-arc' hypothesis fails. Stability instead comes from the long straight contact-edge increasing friction with the substrate. On a slope, pyriform eggs are demonstrably more stable than ellipsoidal eggs (more pyriform ⇒ more stable on slope). The shape is selected for slope-stability, not for blocking rolls.
llm_application: Reframe alignment 'narrow safety basin' (AsFT, 2506.08473) as an analog of the contact-edge stability principle: the parameter region where safety holds is not a thin manifold the model can roll off of; rather it has a long 'contact ridge' along which gradient descent stays on the friction-dominated side. Null-space-traversal: orthogonalize parameter updates to the contact-ridge tangent and apply only the null-space component (perpendicular to the ridge) when the model is far from the safety boundary; switch to projecting onto the ridge tangent (the friction direction) when close. Predicts post-finetuning safety is dominated by 'contact length' (ridge tangent magnitude) rather than basin depth.
novelty_claim: AsFT (2506.08473) anchors safety by penalizing updates orthogonal to alignment direction (the opposite of candidate proposal); Geometry-Aligned LLM Fine-Tuning (2603.16028) constrains trajectories inside workspace boundaries. None proposes the pyriform-egg-style 'contact-ridge length governs stability' framing with a state-dependent switch between null-space and tangent projections. Open question whether 'contact-ridge length' is substantively new or a re-description of basin curvature.
